name: Greek Moussaka
description: Layered eggplant and spiced lamb casserole topped with creamy bechamel sauce
instructions: Slice eggplant into 1cm rounds, salt and let drain for 30 minutes, then pat dry.
Brush eggplant slices with olive oil and roast at 200°C for 20 minutes until golden.
Sauté onion and garlic in olive oil, add ground lamb and cook until browned.
Stir in crushed tomatoes, cinnamon, salt, and pepper; simmer for 15 minutes.
Make bechamel: melt butter, whisk in flour, gradually add milk, stirring until thick.
Remove bechamel from heat, stir in egg and nutmeg.
Layer roasted eggplant, meat sauce, and bechamel in a baking dish.
Bake at 180°C for 40 minutes until top is golden brown. Let rest 15 minutes before serving.
tips: Salting the eggplant removes bitterness and excess moisture.
Let it rest after baking so layers hold together when sliced.

name: Chicken Souvlaki with Tzatziki
description: Herb-marinated grilled chicken skewers served with cool cucumber yogurt sauce
instructions: Cut chicken into 2cm cubes and marinate in olive oil, lemon juice, garlic, and oregano for at least 20 minutes.
Grate cucumber, squeeze out excess water, and mix with yogurt, minced garlic, and salt for tzatziki.
Thread marinated chicken onto skewers.
Grill skewers on high heat for 4-5 minutes per side until charred and cooked through.
Warm pita bread on the grill for 30 seconds per side.
Slice tomato and red onion thinly.
Serve chicken skewers in pita with tzatziki, tomato, and onion.
tips: Marinate overnight for deeper flavor.
Soak wooden skewers in water for 30 minutes before grilling to prevent burning.

name: Spanakopita
description: Flaky phyllo pastry filled with spinach, feta, and herbs
instructions: Wilt spinach in a pan, squeeze out all excess water, and chop roughly.
Sauté onion and scallions in olive oil until soft.
Mix spinach, onion mixture, crumbled feta, eggs, dill, nutmeg, salt, and pepper in a bowl.
Brush a baking dish with olive oil and layer 6 sheets of phyllo, brushing each with oil.
Spread the spinach-feta filling evenly over the phyllo layers.
Top with remaining 6 phyllo sheets, brushing each with oil.
Score the top layers into squares and bake at 180°C for 35-40 minutes until golden and crisp.
tips: Keep phyllo covered with a damp towel while working to prevent drying.
Let rest 10 minutes before cutting for cleaner slices.

name: Falafel with Tahini Sauce
description: Crispy chickpea fritters with herbs served with creamy tahini dressing
instructions: Soak dried chickpeas overnight (do not use canned), then drain well.
Blend chickpeas, onion, garlic, parsley, cilantro, cumin, and coriander in a food processor until coarsely ground.
Add baking powder and salt, pulse to combine. Refrigerate mixture for 1 hour.
Shape mixture into small patties or balls.
Deep fry in hot oil (180°C) for 3-4 minutes until deep golden brown.
Mix tahini with lemon juice, garlic, and water to make sauce.
Serve falafel with tahini sauce, pita, and fresh vegetables.
tips: Never use canned chickpeas for falafel — they are too wet and will fall apart.
If mixture is too wet, add a tablespoon of flour.

name: Shakshuka
description: Eggs poached in a spiced tomato and pepper sauce with cumin and paprika
instructions: Heat olive oil in a deep skillet and sauté diced onion and bell pepper until soft, about 5 minutes.
Add garlic, cumin, paprika, and chili flakes; cook for 1 minute until fragrant.
Pour in crushed tomatoes, season with salt and pepper, and simmer for 10 minutes.
Make 4 wells in the sauce and crack an egg into each one.
Cover and cook on low heat for 6-8 minutes until egg whites are set but yolks are still runny.
Garnish with fresh cilantro and serve directly from the skillet with crusty bread.
tips: For creamier shakshuka, crumble feta cheese over the top before serving.
Cover tightly so the eggs steam-cook evenly.

name: Greek Lemon Chicken
description: Roasted chicken with lemon, garlic, oregano, and golden potatoes in the Greek tradition
instructions: Cut potatoes into wedges and place in a large roasting pan.
Nestle chicken thighs among the potatoes.
Whisk together olive oil, lemon juice, minced garlic, oregano, salt, and pepper.
Pour the lemon mixture over chicken and potatoes, tossing to coat evenly.
Add chicken broth to the pan and tuck lemon halves between pieces.
Roast at 200°C for 60-65 minutes, basting halfway, until chicken is golden and potatoes are crisp.
Let rest 5 minutes before serving with pan juices spooned over the top.
tips: Use bone-in, skin-on thighs for the juiciest result.
The pan juices mixed with lemon make a natural sauce — don't waste them.

name: Lamb Kofta
description: Spiced ground lamb skewers grilled until charred, served with yogurt and flatbread
instructions: Grate onion and squeeze out excess moisture.
Mix ground lamb with grated onion, minced garlic, parsley, cumin, coriander, paprika, cinnamon, and salt.
Knead the mixture well and shape into elongated sausage shapes around skewers.
Refrigerate for 15 minutes to firm up.
Grill kofta over high heat for 3-4 minutes per side until charred and cooked through.
Serve with Greek yogurt, warm pita bread, and a simple salad.
tips: Grating the onion instead of chopping gives a smoother texture.
Don't over-handle the meat or it becomes tough.

name: Fattoush Salad
description: Crispy pita chip salad with fresh vegetables and tangy sumac dressing
instructions: Tear pita bread into bite-sized pieces, brush with olive oil, and toast at 200°C until crispy.
Chop lettuce, dice tomatoes and cucumber, and thinly slice radishes and green onions.
Tear fresh mint leaves.
Whisk together olive oil, lemon juice, pomegranate molasses, sumac, salt, and pepper for the dressing.
Combine all vegetables in a large bowl.
Add crispy pita chips and dressing just before serving, tossing gently.
tips: Add pita chips at the last moment so they stay crispy.
Sumac is essential — it gives the signature tangy flavor.

name: Baba Ganoush with Pita
description: Smoky roasted eggplant dip blended with tahini, lemon, and garlic
instructions: Prick eggplants all over with a fork and roast directly over a gas flame or under the broiler, turning, until skin is charred and flesh is completely soft.
Let eggplants cool, then scoop out the soft flesh and discard skin.
Drain excess liquid from the eggplant flesh.
Mash eggplant with tahini, lemon juice, minced garlic, cumin, and salt.
Transfer to a serving bowl, drizzle with olive oil, and sprinkle with smoked paprika and parsley.
Serve with warm pita bread for dipping.
tips: Charring over an open flame gives the best smoky flavor.
Let the eggplant drain well or the dip will be watery.

name: Grilled Halloumi Salad
description: Warm grilled halloumi cheese over a bed of fresh vegetables with herb vinaigrette
instructions: Slice halloumi into 1cm thick slabs and pat dry.
Grill or pan-sear halloumi on high heat for 2 minutes per side until golden.
Halve cherry tomatoes, dice cucumber, and thinly slice red onion.
Arrange salad greens on plates and top with tomatoes, cucumber, onion, and olives.
Whisk olive oil, lemon juice, oregano, salt, and pepper for the dressing.
Place warm grilled halloumi on top of the salad, drizzle with dressing, and garnish with mint.
tips: Halloumi is best eaten warm while still soft inside.
Pat halloumi dry before grilling for a better sear.

name: Stuffed Grape Leaves (Dolma)
description: Tender grape leaves filled with herbed rice, pine nuts, and currants
instructions: If using jarred grape leaves, rinse well and pat dry. Blanch fresh leaves in boiling water for 1 minute.
Sauté diced onion in olive oil until translucent, then add pine nuts and toast lightly.
Mix rice, sautéed onion, pine nuts, currants, dill, mint, allspice, salt, and a tablespoon of olive oil.
Place a grape leaf shiny-side down, add a tablespoon of filling near the stem end, fold sides in, and roll tightly.
Pack rolls snugly seam-side down in a pot lined with extra grape leaves.
Add lemon juice, remaining olive oil, and enough water to just cover the rolls.
Place a plate on top to weigh them down, cover, and simmer on low for 45 minutes until rice is cooked.
tips: Pack the dolma tightly so they don't unravel during cooking.
Serve cold with lemon wedges for the traditional meze presentation.

name: Mediterranean Fish Stew
description: Hearty tomato-saffron broth with mixed white fish, shrimp, and fennel
instructions: Bloom saffron threads in 2 tablespoons of warm water for 10 minutes.
Sauté sliced fennel and diced onion in olive oil until softened, about 5 minutes.
Add garlic and cook 1 minute, then pour in white wine and let it reduce by half.
Add crushed tomatoes, saffron water, salt, and pepper; simmer for 15 minutes.
Cut fish into large chunks, add to the stew, and cook for 5 minutes.
Add shrimp and cook 3-4 minutes more until shrimp are pink and fish flakes easily.
Ladle into bowls, garnish with fresh parsley, and serve with crusty bread.
tips: Don't stir aggressively or the fish will break apart.
A splash of Pernod or ouzo at the end adds an authentic anise note.

name: Tabbouleh
description: Fresh parsley and bulgur wheat salad with tomatoes, mint, and lemon dressing
instructions: Soak bulgur in boiling water for 15 minutes, then drain well and squeeze out excess water.
Finely chop parsley and mint leaves — the herbs should dominate, not the bulgur.
Dice tomatoes and finely slice green onions.
Combine bulgur, herbs, tomatoes, and onions in a large bowl.
Whisk together lemon juice, olive oil, salt, and pepper.
Pour dressing over salad and toss. Let sit 10 minutes before serving for flavors to meld.
tips: Traditional tabbouleh is mostly herbs with just a little bulgur.
Use flat-leaf parsley for the most authentic flavor.

name: Hummus Bowl with Roasted Vegetables
description: Creamy hummus base topped with roasted seasonal vegetables, chickpeas, and olive oil
instructions: Blend chickpeas (reserve a few for topping), tahini, lemon juice, garlic, cumin, and 2 tbsp ice water until very smooth.
Cut zucchini, bell pepper, and red onion into chunks; halve the cherry tomatoes.
Toss vegetables with olive oil, paprika, salt, and pepper.
Roast vegetables at 200°C for 20-25 minutes until caramelized.
Spread hummus generously on plates, top with roasted vegetables and reserved whole chickpeas.
Drizzle with olive oil and scatter toasted pine nuts over the top.
tips: Add ice water to the hummus for an ultra-creamy texture.
A pinch of baking soda in the chickpeas before blending makes it even silkier.

name: Greek Gigantes (Baked Giant Beans)
description: Giant white beans slow-baked in a rich tomato sauce with herbs and olive oil
instructions: If using dried beans, soak overnight and boil until just tender (about 45 minutes). Drain.
Sauté diced onion, carrot, and celery in olive oil until soft.
Add garlic and cook 1 minute, then add crushed tomatoes, sugar, salt, and pepper.
Simmer sauce for 10 minutes, then stir in the beans.
Transfer to a baking dish and drizzle generously with olive oil.
Bake uncovered at 180°C for 45-50 minutes until sauce is thick and top is slightly caramelized.
Garnish with fresh parsley and serve warm with crusty bread.
tips: The long bake is key — it concentrates the sauce and makes the beans creamy inside.
Use good quality olive oil as it is a starring ingredient here.

name: Imam Bayildi (Stuffed Eggplant)
description: Whole eggplant stuffed with onions, tomatoes, and garlic braised in olive oil
instructions: Cut a deep slit lengthwise in each eggplant without cutting through. Salt the insides and let drain for 20 minutes.
Sauté thinly sliced onions in olive oil over low heat until very soft and golden, about 15 minutes.
Add sliced garlic, diced tomatoes, parsley, sugar, salt, and pepper to the onions; cook 5 minutes.
Rinse and pat dry the eggplants, then stuff the filling into each slit.
Arrange stuffed eggplants in a deep pan, pour any remaining filling around them.
Add lemon juice and enough water to come halfway up the eggplants.
Cover and braise on low heat for 40 minutes until eggplant is completely tender. Serve warm or at room temperature.
tips: This dish is traditionally served at room temperature.
The generous olive oil is essential — do not reduce it.

name: Moroccan Tagine with Chickpeas
description: Aromatic North African stew with chickpeas, apricots, and warm spices
instructions: Sauté diced onion in olive oil until golden, then add garlic, cumin, cinnamon, and turmeric.
Add cubed sweet potato and stir to coat with the spices.
Pour in diced tomatoes and enough water to just cover. Bring to a simmer.
Add drained chickpeas and chopped dried apricots.
Cover and simmer for 30 minutes until sweet potato is tender and sauce is thick.
Meanwhile, prepare couscous according to package directions.
Serve the tagine over fluffy couscous, garnished with fresh cilantro.
tips: The apricots add essential sweetness — don't skip them.
A drizzle of honey and a handful of toasted almonds elevate this dish further.

name: Mediterranean Lentil Soup
description: Warming red lentil soup with cumin, lemon, and a swirl of olive oil
instructions: Sauté diced onion and carrot in olive oil until softened, about 5 minutes.
Add garlic, cumin, and turmeric; cook for 1 minute until fragrant.
Add rinsed red lentils and vegetable broth. Bring to a boil.
Reduce heat and simmer for 20-25 minutes until lentils are completely soft.
Blend half the soup for a creamy texture while keeping some lentils whole.
Stir in lemon juice, season with salt and pepper.
Serve drizzled with olive oil and a lemon wedge on the side.
tips: Red lentils don't need soaking and cook very quickly.
The lemon juice at the end brightens the whole soup — add it generously.

name: Grilled Swordfish with Capers
description: Thick swordfish steaks grilled and topped with a lemon-caper-olive relish
instructions: Pat swordfish steaks dry, brush with olive oil, and season with salt and pepper.
Grill swordfish on high heat for 4 minutes per side until charred outside and opaque inside.
Meanwhile, make the relish: combine halved cherry tomatoes, chopped olives, capers, minced garlic, oregano, lemon zest, and olive oil.
Let the swordfish rest for 2 minutes after grilling.
Spoon the caper-olive relish generously over the fish.
Serve with a squeeze of fresh lemon juice.
tips: Don't overcook swordfish — it dries out quickly past medium.
Oil the grill well to prevent sticking.

name: Panzanella (Tuscan Bread Salad)
description: Rustic Italian bread salad with ripe tomatoes, basil, and red wine vinaigrette
instructions: Tear stale bread into rough chunks and drizzle with a little water and olive oil if very hard.
Cut tomatoes into wedges, dice cucumber, and thinly slice red onion.
Whisk together olive oil, red wine vinegar, salt, and pepper.
Combine bread, tomatoes, cucumber, onion, and capers in a large bowl.
Pour dressing over and toss well. Let sit for 15 minutes so the bread absorbs the juices.
Tear fresh basil over the top and toss once more before serving.
tips: Stale bread is essential — fresh bread gets too mushy.
Make this at peak tomato season for the best flavor.

name: Pastitsio (Greek Baked Pasta)
description: Greek-style baked pasta with spiced meat sauce and creamy bechamel topping
instructions: Cook penne until al dente, drain and toss with a little olive oil.
Brown ground beef with onion and garlic, then add crushed tomatoes, cinnamon, salt, and pepper. Simmer 15 minutes.
Make bechamel: melt butter, whisk in flour, gradually add milk, stirring until thick and smooth.
Remove bechamel from heat, stir in beaten eggs and nutmeg.
Layer half the pasta in a greased baking dish, spread all the meat sauce over it, then top with remaining pasta.
Pour bechamel evenly over the top.
Bake at 180°C for 40-45 minutes until golden brown. Let rest 20 minutes before cutting.
tips: The resting time is crucial — it lets layers set for clean slices.
The cinnamon in the meat sauce is what makes it distinctly Greek.

name: Shrimp Saganaki
description: Plump shrimp baked in a spiced tomato sauce with crumbled feta cheese
instructions: Sauté diced onion in olive oil until soft, add garlic and cook 1 minute.
Add crushed tomatoes, oregano, chili flakes, salt, and pepper; simmer for 10 minutes.
Add ouzo and stir to combine.
Nestle peeled shrimp into the sauce in a single layer.
Crumble feta cheese over the top.
Bake at 200°C for 10-12 minutes until shrimp are pink and feta is slightly melted.
Garnish with parsley and serve with crusty bread to soak up the sauce.
tips: Ouzo adds authentic flavor, but you can substitute with a splash of white wine.
Don't overcook the shrimp — they cook quickly in the hot sauce.

name: Roasted Red Pepper and Walnut Dip (Muhammara)
description: Smoky-sweet Syrian dip of roasted red peppers, walnuts, and pomegranate molasses
instructions: Toast walnuts in a dry pan over medium heat until fragrant, about 3 minutes.
Pulse roasted red peppers, toasted walnuts, breadcrumbs, garlic, and cumin in a food processor.
Add pomegranate molasses, olive oil, Aleppo pepper, lemon juice, and salt.
Blend until you have a coarse, spreadable consistency — not completely smooth.
Transfer to a serving bowl, drizzle with olive oil, and sprinkle with extra Aleppo pepper.
Serve with warm pita bread for dipping.
tips: Aleppo pepper is milder than regular chili flakes — it adds warmth without harsh heat.
This dip tastes even better the next day after flavors meld.

name: Za'atar Roasted Chicken
description: Whole spatchcocked chicken rubbed with za'atar and roasted until golden and crispy
instructions: Spatchcock the chicken by removing the backbone and pressing flat.
Mix za'atar, olive oil, lemon juice, sumac, minced garlic, salt, and pepper into a paste.
Rub the za'atar paste all over the chicken, including under the skin.
Place sliced onion and lemon halves in a roasting pan and set chicken on top, skin-side up.
Roast at 220°C for 45-50 minutes until skin is golden and crisp, and internal temperature reaches 75°C.
Rest for 10 minutes before carving. Spoon pan juices over the chicken.
tips: Spatchcocking ensures even cooking and crispier skin.
Za'atar quality varies — use a fresh, well-made blend with lots of sumac and sesame.

name: Mediterranean Orzo Salad
description: Orzo pasta tossed with sun-dried tomatoes, olives, feta, and herb vinaigrette
instructions: Cook orzo according to package directions. Drain, rinse with cold water, and drain again.
Chop sun-dried tomatoes, halve olives, dice cucumber and bell pepper, and finely slice red onion.
Whisk together olive oil, lemon juice, oregano, salt, and pepper.
Toss cooled orzo with all the vegetables and dressing.
Crumble feta cheese over the top and tear fresh basil leaves over it.
Toss gently and serve at room temperature.
tips: Rinse orzo well with cold water to prevent clumping.
This salad improves after 30 minutes in the fridge as flavors meld.

name: Harissa Grilled Lamb Chops
description: Tender lamb chops marinated in fiery harissa paste and grilled until charred
instructions: Mix harissa paste, olive oil, minced garlic, lemon juice, cumin, honey, and salt into a marinade.
Coat lamb chops thoroughly in the marinade and refrigerate for at least 30 minutes.
Bring lamb to room temperature before grilling.
Grill on high heat for 3-4 minutes per side for medium-rare.
Let rest for 5 minutes before serving.
Mix yogurt with chopped mint for a cooling sauce and serve alongside the chops.
tips: Don't skip resting the meat — it redistributes juices for better flavor.
Harissa varies in heat — taste first and adjust quantity.

name: Turkish Lentil Soup (Mercimek)
description: Velvety smooth red lentil soup with a drizzle of paprika butter, a Turkish staple
instructions: Sauté diced onion, carrot, and potato in a splash of oil until slightly softened.
Add garlic, tomato paste, and cumin; cook for 1 minute.
Add rinsed red lentils and vegetable broth. Bring to a boil.
Reduce heat and simmer for 20-25 minutes until lentils and vegetables are very soft.
Blend the soup until completely smooth with an immersion blender.
Melt butter in a small pan with paprika to make a paprika butter drizzle.
Serve soup in bowls with paprika butter swirled on top and lemon wedges on the side.
tips: The paprika butter drizzle on top is traditional and essential.
Blend until very smooth — Mercimek should be velvety, not chunky.

name: Greek Bean Soup (Fasolada)
description: Hearty national dish of Greece — white beans simmered with tomatoes, celery, and olive oil
instructions: Soak beans overnight in plenty of water. Drain and boil in fresh water for 20 minutes, then drain.
In a large pot, sauté diced onion, carrots, and celery in olive oil for 5 minutes.
Add garlic and cook 1 minute.
Add par-boiled beans, crushed tomatoes, bay leaves, and enough water to cover by 2 inches.
Bring to a boil, reduce heat, and simmer for 45-60 minutes until beans are creamy and tender.
Season with salt and pepper, remove bay leaves.
Serve in bowls with a generous drizzle of olive oil and fresh parsley.
tips: The generous olive oil stirred in at the end is what makes this dish special.
Fasolada is even better reheated the next day.

name: Grilled Octopus with Lemon
description: Tender braised then grilled octopus with lemon, olive oil, and oregano
instructions: Clean octopus and place in a pot with bay leaf — do not add water. Cover tightly.
Simmer over low heat for 45-60 minutes until tender (octopus releases its own liquid).
Drain and let cool slightly, then cut into portions.
Toss octopus pieces with olive oil, garlic, oregano, salt, and pepper.
Grill on very high heat for 2-3 minutes per side until charred at the edges.
Dress with lemon juice, red wine vinegar, capers, and fresh parsley.
Serve immediately while warm.
tips: The braising step is essential — without it the octopus will be rubbery.
No water needed when braising — the octopus releases enough liquid.

name: Caponata (Sicilian Eggplant Stew)
description: Sweet and sour Sicilian eggplant stew with tomatoes, capers, olives, and pine nuts
instructions: Cut eggplant into 2cm cubes, salt, and let drain for 20 minutes. Pat dry.
Fry eggplant cubes in olive oil until golden on all sides. Set aside on paper towels.
In the same pan, sauté diced celery and onion until soft.
Add crushed tomatoes, olives, capers, and pine nuts; simmer for 10 minutes.
Stir in vinegar and sugar to create the sweet-sour balance.
Return eggplant to the sauce, fold gently, and cook 5 more minutes.
Let cool to room temperature, garnish with fresh basil, and serve.
tips: Caponata is best served at room temperature or cold.
The sweet-sour balance is the signature — adjust vinegar and sugar to taste.

name: Couscous with Seven Vegetables
description: Traditional North African couscous with a fragrant seven-vegetable stew and chickpeas
instructions: Sauté diced onion in olive oil until golden, then add ras el hanout and stir 1 minute.
Add chopped carrots, turnip, and sweet potato — the harder vegetables first.
Add enough water to cover generously, bring to a boil, and simmer for 15 minutes.
Add zucchini, cabbage, diced tomatoes, and drained chickpeas. Simmer 15 more minutes.
Meanwhile, prepare couscous: pour 2 cups boiling broth over couscous, cover, and let sit 5 minutes, then fluff.
Season the stew with salt and pepper. The broth should be rich but thin enough to ladle.
Mound couscous on a platter, make a well in the center, and ladle the vegetable stew over it. Garnish with cilantro.
tips: Ras el hanout is essential — it is a complex spice blend that defines this dish.
Traditionally the broth is served alongside in a bowl for adding to taste.

name: Lahmacun (Turkish Flatbread Pizza)
description: Paper-thin crispy flatbread topped with spiced lamb, tomatoes, and fresh herbs
instructions: Mix flour, yeast, salt, and warm water to form a soft dough. Knead 5 minutes and let rest 30 minutes.
Finely mince (or process) the lamb, onion, tomatoes, and bell pepper together until almost paste-like.
Mix the meat mixture with tomato paste, Aleppo pepper, cumin, and salt.
Divide dough into 8 portions and roll each paper-thin into rounds.
Spread a thin layer of the meat mixture over each round, all the way to the edges.
Bake at 250°C on a preheated baking stone for 5-7 minutes until edges are crisp.
Serve with fresh parsley, a squeeze of lemon, and roll up to eat.
tips: Roll the dough as thin as possible — it should be see-through almost.
The meat topping should be spread very thin, like a paste, not a chunky layer.

name: Gemista (Greek Stuffed Tomatoes)
description: Ripe tomatoes stuffed with herbed rice and baked until caramelized
instructions: Cut tops off tomatoes and scoop out the pulp. Reserve pulp and chop it.
Sprinkle a little sugar inside each tomato shell.
Sauté onion in olive oil until soft, add garlic, rice, pine nuts, herbs, reserved tomato pulp, salt, and pepper.
Cook for 2 minutes, then stuff each tomato with the rice mixture, leaving room for rice to expand.
Place caps back on tomatoes and arrange in a baking dish.
Tuck potato wedges between tomatoes, drizzle everything with olive oil, and add a little water to the dish.
Bake at 180°C for 55-60 minutes until rice is cooked and tomatoes are soft and caramelized.
tips: Leave space in the tomatoes — the rice expands significantly.
The potatoes cook in the tomato juices and become incredibly flavorful.

name: Mediterranean Chickpea Stew
description: Comforting chickpea stew with spinach, tomatoes, and warm spices
instructions: Sauté diced onion in olive oil until golden, about 5 minutes.
Add garlic, cumin, and smoked paprika; cook 1 minute.
Add diced tomatoes, drained chickpeas, and vegetable broth.
Simmer for 15 minutes until sauce thickens.
Stir in spinach and cook until wilted, about 2 minutes.
Finish with lemon juice, salt, and pepper.
Serve drizzled with olive oil and warm bread on the side.
tips: Smoked paprika gives a depth that regular paprika can't match.
Mash a few chickpeas against the pot to thicken the stew naturally.

name: Grilled Sea Bass with Herbs
description: Whole sea bass grilled with lemon, olive oil, and Mediterranean herbs
instructions: Score each sea bass 3 times on each side with a sharp knife.
Rub fish inside and out with olive oil, salt, and pepper.
Stuff the cavity of each fish with lemon slices, garlic, rosemary, and thyme.
Grill on medium-high heat for 5-6 minutes per side until skin is crispy and flesh flakes easily.
Meanwhile, blister cherry tomatoes in a hot pan with a drizzle of olive oil.
Serve fish whole with blistered tomatoes, fresh parsley, and extra lemon wedges.
tips: Scoring helps the fish cook evenly and lets flavors penetrate.
Oil the grill grates generously to prevent skin from sticking.

name: Horiatiki (Greek Village Salad)
description: Classic Greek salad with chunky tomatoes, cucumber, olives, and a slab of feta
instructions: Cut tomatoes into large wedges — do not dice them small.
Peel and cut cucumber into thick half-moons.
Slice green pepper into rings and red onion into thin slices.
Arrange vegetables on a plate or shallow bowl.
Scatter Kalamata olives over the vegetables.
Place a thick slab of feta cheese on top — do not crumble it.
Drizzle generously with olive oil, sprinkle with oregano, add a splash of vinegar, and season with salt.
tips: A true Horiatiki has no lettuce — just chunky vegetables.
Place the feta as a whole slab on top — crumbling it is not traditional.

name: Karniyarik (Stuffed Eggplant with Meat)
description: Turkish split eggplant filled with spiced ground beef, tomatoes, and peppers
instructions: Peel eggplants in stripes (leaving some skin) and fry in oil until golden. Drain on paper towels.
Sauté diced onion until golden, add ground beef and cook until browned.
Add garlic, diced pepper, one chopped tomato, tomato paste, allspice, and salt. Cook 5 minutes.
Make a deep slit in each fried eggplant and open like a pocket.
Fill each eggplant with the meat mixture and place in a baking dish.
Slice remaining tomato and place a slice on top of each stuffed eggplant.
Add a little water to the dish, cover with foil, and bake at 180°C for 25 minutes.
tips: Frying the eggplant first gives it a silky texture inside.
The name means 'split belly' — the slit should be generous to hold plenty of filling.

name: Kibbeh (Bulgur and Lamb Croquettes)
description: Crispy fried bulgur and lamb shells stuffed with spiced meat, pine nuts, and onions
instructions: Soak bulgur in warm water for 15 minutes, then drain and squeeze dry.
For the shell: blend half the lamb with the soaked bulgur, grated onion, allspice, salt, and pepper until smooth and paste-like.
For the filling: sauté diced onion, remaining ground lamb, pine nuts, cinnamon, cumin, and salt until cooked through.
Take a handful of shell mixture, form into an egg shape, hollow it out, stuff with filling, and pinch closed into a torpedo shape.
Repeat until all mixture is used.
Deep fry in hot oil (180°C) for 4-5 minutes until deep golden brown and crispy.
Drain on paper towels and serve hot with yogurt and fresh mint.
tips: Keep your hands wet while shaping to prevent the mixture from sticking.
The shell mixture must be very smooth — process it well or the kibbeh will crack when frying.

name: Mediterranean Roasted Vegetable Platter
description: Assorted seasonal vegetables roasted with olive oil, garlic, and za'atar
instructions: Cut all vegetables into large, similar-sized pieces for even roasting.
Toss vegetables with olive oil, za'atar, whole garlic cloves, and salt.
Spread in a single layer on a large baking sheet — don't overcrowd.
Roast at 220°C for 30-35 minutes, stirring once halfway, until charred at the edges.
Squeeze lemon juice over the hot roasted vegetables.
Arrange on a platter, garnish with fresh parsley, and drizzle with extra olive oil.
Serve warm or at room temperature with hummus and pita.
tips: Don't overcrowd the pan — the vegetables need space to roast, not steam.
Za'atar gives a wonderful earthy, tangy crust to the vegetables.

name: Turkish Pide (Stuffed Flatbread)
description: Boat-shaped Turkish flatbread filled with spiced ground meat, peppers, and tomatoes
instructions: Mix flour, yeast, salt, and warm water to form a soft dough. Knead 8 minutes and let rise 45 minutes.
Mix ground beef with finely diced onion, pepper, tomato, tomato paste, cumin, Aleppo pepper, and salt.
Divide dough into 4 portions and roll each into an oval shape.
Spread meat filling down the center of each oval, leaving borders.
Fold and pinch the long edges up to form a boat shape with pointed ends.
Brush edges with egg yolk and bake at 230°C for 12-15 minutes until golden.
Brush hot pide with melted butter immediately after removing from the oven.
tips: The buttering at the end is traditional and makes the crust incredibly tender.
Pinch the ends tightly to maintain the boat shape during baking.

name: Saag-Style Mediterranean Greens with Feta
description: Slow-cooked mixed greens with garlic, olive oil, and tangy crumbled feta
instructions: Wash and roughly chop spinach and Swiss chard, removing tough stems.
Heat olive oil in a large pan and sauté sliced garlic until golden, about 1 minute.
Add the greens in batches, stirring as they wilt down.
Season with salt, chili flakes, and nutmeg. Cook on low for 15 minutes until very tender.
Squeeze in lemon juice and stir.
Transfer to a serving dish and crumble feta generously over the top.
Serve warm with crusty bread for scooping.
tips: Mixing different greens gives more complex flavor than spinach alone.
Cook the greens down well — they should be silky, not just wilted.

name: Grilled Chicken Shawarma Plate
description: Spiced grilled chicken shawarma served with garlic sauce, pickles, and warm flatbread
instructions: Marinate chicken thighs in yogurt, cumin, turmeric, paprika, cinnamon, minced garlic, lemon juice, and olive oil for at least 1 hour.
Grill chicken on medium-high heat for 6-7 minutes per side until charred and cooked through.
Let chicken rest for 5 minutes, then slice thinly against the grain.
Make garlic sauce: whisk tahini with lemon juice, minced garlic, and water until smooth.
Warm pita bread on the grill for 30 seconds per side.
Arrange sliced chicken on plates with warm pita, pickled turnips, and garlic tahini sauce.
tips: The yogurt marinade tenderizes the chicken and helps the spices adhere.
Bone-in, skin-on thighs give the most flavorful shawarma.

name: White Bean and Tomato Stew
description: Rustic one-pot stew of creamy white beans in a garlicky tomato broth with herbs
instructions: Sauté diced onion, carrot, and celery in olive oil until softened, about 5 minutes.
Add garlic and rosemary sprig; cook 1 minute until fragrant.
Add crushed tomatoes, vegetable broth, and drained beans.
Bring to a simmer and cook for 20 minutes until sauce thickens.
Mash a few beans against the side of the pot to thicken the stew.
Remove rosemary sprig, season with salt and pepper.
Serve in bowls with a generous drizzle of olive oil and fresh parsley.
tips: Mashing some of the beans creates a naturally creamy stew without any dairy.
A drizzle of your best olive oil at the end makes all the difference.

name: Olive Oil Poached Fish with Tomatoes
description: Delicate white fish gently poached in olive oil with cherry tomatoes, garlic, and capers
instructions: Heat olive oil in a wide, deep skillet over medium-low heat.
Add sliced garlic and cook gently until golden, about 1 minute.
Add halved cherry tomatoes, capers, and olives; cook until tomatoes begin to soften.
Pour in white wine and let it simmer for 2 minutes.
Season fish fillets with salt and pepper, then nestle them into the tomato mixture.
Spoon the olive oil and tomato mixture over the fish, cover, and poach on low for 8-10 minutes until fish is opaque and flakes easily.
Garnish with fresh basil and lemon zest, and serve with the poaching oil spooned over the fish.
tips: Low and slow is the key — high heat will toughen the fish.
The infused olive oil is part of the dish — serve it as a sauce.
